body_txt: Specialization is a common trend in fiction. It allows the author to create an ensemble cast where every member of a team provides a specific function, and avoids role duplication. This character says " specialization is for insects! " Perhaps a character is a loner and needs to be able to do everything on their own. Perhaps everyone else is so useless, they had to take on all of the jobs. Perhaps they just have a wide variety of interests. Perhaps there is no room or money to hire a team of specialists and this character was a necessary compromise. May be a sign of a person with a checkered past, who has had to take on a lot of roles in their life, just to get by. Whatever the reason, this character has at least some skill in a wide variety of disciplines. Sometimes The Leader of a team may be a Jack-Of-All-Trades, with a good, basic grasp of the specialized skills possessed by the members of their team, allowing them to understand how to use each team member to their fullest potential. It could be argued that being good at everything is a form of specialization. While a jack-of-all character might not stand out, they are usually well-equipped to handle most situations and most opponents. It's their well-rounded nature that makes them so versatile allowing them to deal with almost anything or sub in for anyone at the last second. What if the opponent is really strong but not very fast? The jack-of-all can outpace them while being strong enough to do damage. What if the opponent is faster? Usually the jack-of-all is fast enough to defend against if not avoid their opponent's attacks and deal out more punishment in a war of attrition. Which trades are filled might determinate how much the jack-of-all can be effective and useful in a scenario. Not all skills have the same impact, depending on the context. Sometimes, the skills blend in synergy, allowing for a competent character that can go deeper than someone who mastered only one. Again, it could be argued that the crossfield between two trades can open for a new specialization. A warrior who is trained enough in swordsmanship, polearms, axes, will be able to fight in different conditions and adapt after breaking one weapon; instead, a warrior who spent time training in swordsmanship and professional poker won't be more useful for an army, or an RPG party when it's time to fight. A scientist who studied microbiology and botany will have benefits in a research project involving the interactions between bacteria and plants; instead, a scientist who studied two totally unrelated disciplines such as anthropology and electrical engineering doesn't have much to put together, and likely in both fields won't catch up with someone who mastered one or the other. The inversion might also be true . Perhaps the warrior competent with swords, axes and polearms ends up with redundant skills, because roles are defined and (s)he will only stick to swords, while someone else is using halberds, and there is no need for swapping. Instead, a warrior who trained only in swords, and has some bard skills, can still end up useful as a minor diplomat or in getting discounts from vendors, when you can't or don't want to have a pure bard in the party for any reason. The anthropologist who also studied electrical engineering managed to find a new job when the department closed due to the economic crisis, he or she simply changed job. That's why it is important to define contexts and conditions when judging the capabilities of someone. Supertrope of Renaissance Man (where someone is exceptionally good at many things) and Master of None (where someone can do a lot of things to the bare minimum and has no standout skill). A related trope found in Video Games and Tabletop Games is the Jack of All Stats , who has well-balanced stats, not skills. When this trope is applied to the human species as a whole, you get Humans Are Average . See also The Red Mage , Combo Platter Powers , and Power Copying . Do-Anything Robot may be a justification for this. Compare the Master of All , who is the best (or among the best) in practically everything. Contrast Min-Maxing , Tactical Rock–Paper–Scissors , Elemental Rock–Paper–Scissors , Crippling Overspecialization , Sacrificed Basic Skill for Awesome Training . For similar tropes related to specific fields, see Omnidisciplinary Lawyer , Do-Anything Soldier , and Omnidisciplinary Scientist . In the binary of Quantity vs. Quality , this trope sides with quantity. When adding examples, be careful not to confuse with Jack of All Stats . That trope involves stats or abilities, while this trope involves skills or roles.
